question: What is the difference between a class and an object in Java?
correct: A class is the blueprint; an object is a specific instance created from that blueprint
incorrect: A class is created at runtime; an object exists only at compile time
A class holds values; an object defines behavior
A class and an object are two names for the same thing
feedback: A class defines the structure (fields) and behavior (methods). An object is a concrete instance in memory. You can create many objects from one class â€” like many houses built from one blueprint.

question: What keyword is used to create a new object in Java?
correct: new
incorrect: create
object
make
feedback: new allocates memory on the heap and calls the constructor: Dog rex = new Dog("Rex"); The variable rex holds a reference to the new object. The constructor initializes the object's fields.

question: What is encapsulation in Java?
correct: Hiding an object's internal data behind private fields and controlling access through public methods
incorrect: Bundling multiple classes into a single Java file
Ensuring a class can only be instantiated once
Preventing a class from being extended
feedback: Encapsulation protects object state: fields are private (hidden), and access is provided through public getter and setter methods. Setters can validate data before storing it, preventing invalid states.

question: What does the this keyword refer to inside an instance method or constructor?
correct: The current object on which the method was called
incorrect: The parent class of the current object
The class definition itself (not any particular instance)
The most recently created object of this class
feedback: Inside an instance method, this refers to the specific object the method was called on. It is most often used in constructors to disambiguate when a parameter has the same name as a field: this.name = name;

question: What does a constructor do in Java?
correct: Initializes a new object's fields when the object is created with new
incorrect: Creates multiple copies of an existing object
Runs automatically whenever a method is called on the object
Destroys an object when it is no longer needed
feedback: A constructor has the same name as the class and no return type. It runs exactly once â€” when the object is created with new â€” and sets up the initial state of the object.

question: What access modifier makes a field accessible only within the class that declares it?
correct: private
incorrect: public
protected
default (package-private)
feedback: private restricts access to the declaring class only. This is the standard choice for fields in well-designed classes (encapsulation). public allows access from everywhere; protected from subclasses and the same package; default from the same package.

question: What does a getter method do?
correct: Returns the value of a private field to the caller
incorrect: Sets the value of a field and validates the new value
Deletes the field from the object after returning its value
Creates a new object with the same field values
feedback: Getters are typically public methods that return a private field: public String getName() { return name; } They provide controlled read access without exposing the field directly.

question: What is the default toString() output for an object that doesn't override it?
correct: The class name followed by an @ sign and the object's hash code in hexadecimal
incorrect: A JSON representation of all the object's fields
The class name followed by all field values in order
null, since the default implementation is empty
feedback: Object's default toString() returns something like 'Dog@1a2b3c'. This is rarely useful, which is why overriding toString() to return a human-readable description is strongly encouraged.

question: What are the four pillars of object-oriented programming?
correct: Encapsulation, abstraction, inheritance, polymorphism
incorrect: Encapsulation, recursion, generics, polymorphism
Abstraction, compilation, interfaces, constructors
Inheritance, loops, methods, classes
feedback: The four OOP pillars: Encapsulation (hiding implementation), Abstraction (simplifying interfaces), Inheritance (reusing and extending classes), Polymorphism (treating related objects uniformly).

question: What happens to each object's fields when multiple objects are created from the same class?
correct: Each object has its own independent copy of every instance field
incorrect: All objects share the same field values stored in the class
The last object created overwrites the fields of all previous ones
Only the first object created gets initialized fields; the rest default to zero
feedback: Instance fields belong to each individual object. Changing dog1.name does not affect dog2.name. Static fields are the exception â€” they are shared across all instances of the class.

question: Which claims about constructors are correct?
correct: A constructor has the same name as its class
A constructor has no return type (not even void)
If no constructor is defined, Java provides a default no-argument constructor
incorrect: A constructor can be declared with a void return type
A class can have at most one constructor
feedback: Constructors are identified by having the same name as the class and no return type. Multiple constructors are allowed (constructor overloading) as long as their parameter lists differ. Once you define any constructor, the default no-arg constructor is no longer provided automatically.

question: Which claims about private fields and encapsulation are correct?
correct: Private fields cannot be accessed directly from code outside the class
Setter methods can validate new values before storing them
Getters and setters provide controlled public access to private data
incorrect: Private fields are automatically initialized to null in every object
Encapsulation prevents the object from calling its own methods
feedback: Private fields hide implementation details. Setters enforce constraints (e.g., reject a negative age). Getters expose data safely. Private fields of reference types default to null only for instance fields, not local variables.

question: Which claims about the this keyword are correct?
correct: this refers to the current object instance
this is commonly used in constructors when the parameter name shadows the field name
this can be used to call another constructor in the same class using this()
incorrect: this can only be used inside static methods
this refers to the class, not any individual instance
feedback: this is only available in instance (non-static) methods and constructors. this() in a constructor delegates to another constructor in the same class and must be the first statement. static methods have no current instance, so this cannot be used there.

question: Which claims about getter and setter methods are correct?
correct: Getters are typically public, return the field's type, and take no parameters
Setters are typically public, return void, and take one parameter
Setters allow validation logic before modifying the field
incorrect: Getters must always return exactly the raw field value with no modification
A setter must exist for every field in a class
feedback: Getters can compute or format a value before returning it (e.g., return firstName + " " + lastName). Not every field needs a setter â€” immutable fields set in the constructor need no setter. Validation in setters is a key benefit of encapsulation.

question: Which claims about instance fields vs local variables are correct?
correct: Instance fields persist for the lifetime of the object
Local variables exist only during the execution of the method where they are declared
Instance fields of reference type are initialized to null by default; local variables are not initialized automatically
incorrect: Local variables and instance fields both persist across multiple method calls
Instance fields are always faster to access than local variables
feedback: Instance fields store the object's long-term state. Local variables are temporary. The JVM initializes instance fields to defaults (null, 0, false) but forces you to initialize local variables before use.

question: Which of the following are true about the @Override annotation?
correct: It tells the compiler to verify that the annotated method actually overrides a method in a parent class
Forgetting @Override does not break overriding â€” it only removes the compiler safety check
@Override is commonly placed on overridden toString(), equals(), and hashCode() methods
incorrect: @Override causes the method to replace the parent's method everywhere in the program
@Override is required for the override to work at runtime
feedback: @Override is optional but highly recommended. Without it, a typo in the method name produces a new method rather than an override, silently producing bugs. With @Override, the compiler catches the mistake immediately.

question: Which claims about OOP benefits over purely procedural code are correct?
correct: OOP bundles related data and behavior into a single class, improving organization
Objects can enforce invariants through private fields and validation methods
Classes can be reused and extended through inheritance
incorrect: OOP programs always run faster than equivalent procedural programs
OOP eliminates the need for loops and conditionals
feedback: OOP improves organization, safety (invariants), and reusability. It doesn't have inherent performance advantages over procedural code and doesn't change the need for control flow. The benefits are mainly in maintainability and design scalability.

question: The keyword used to create a new object and invoke its constructor is ___.
correct: new
feedback: new allocates heap memory and calls the constructor: Dog d = new Dog("Rex"); Without new, you would only have a variable holding null.

question: A method that returns the value of a private field is called a ___.
correct: getter
accessor
feedback: Getters (also called accessors) expose private field values safely. Standard naming: getName() for a field called name. They return the field's type and take no parameters.

question: A method that allows the caller to change the value of a private field is called a ___.
correct: setter
mutator
feedback: Setters (also called mutators) accept a new value and optionally validate it before storing. Standard naming: setName(String name). They return void.

question: Inside a constructor, this.___ = name; uses the keyword ___ to refer to the current object's field when a parameter has the same name.
correct: this
feedback: this distinguishes the instance field (this.name) from the constructor parameter (name) when they share the same identifier. Without this, name = name is a no-op assignment (parameter to itself).

question: The access modifier that restricts a field to be readable and writable only within the same class is ___.
correct: private
feedback: private is the most restrictive modifier. It enforces encapsulation by hiding fields from all outside code, forcing access through getter/setter methods.

question: When toString() is not overridden, Java calls the default version from the ___ class.
correct: Object
feedback: Every Java class implicitly extends Object. Object.toString() returns ClassName@hashCode in hexadecimal. Override it to return a meaningful string representation of your object.

question: Match each OOP term to its definition.

pairs.definition: A blueprint defining fields and methods shared by all instances
A specific instance of a class created with the new keyword
A special method that runs when an object is created to initialize its fields
Hiding fields as private and exposing them through public methods

feedback: Class is the template; object is the thing built from it. The constructor sets up each new object. Encapsulation protects the object's state.

question: Match each access modifier to its visibility scope.

pairs.definition: Accessible from any class in any package
Accessible only within the same class
Accessible within the same package and by subclasses
Accessible only within the same package (no keyword specified)

feedback: From most to least restrictive: private < default < protected < public. Fields are typically private; public API methods are public; utility methods shared with subclasses use protected.

question: Match each OOP pillar to what it describes.

pairs.definition: Hiding implementation details and exposing a clean public interface
Simplifying complex systems by exposing only essential behavior
Creating new classes that reuse and extend existing class behavior
Using a parent type reference to invoke overridden behavior of a subclass

feedback: The four pillars work together: Encapsulation protects state, Abstraction hides complexity, Inheritance enables reuse, Polymorphism enables flexibility. All are related but describe different aspects of OOP design.

question: Match each method type to its typical signature format.

pairs.definition: public FieldType getFieldName() â€” returns the field, no parameters
public void setFieldName(FieldType newValue) â€” void, one parameter
public ClassName(params) â€” same name as class, no return type
public String toString() â€” returns a text description of the object

feedback: These four method patterns appear in nearly every Java class. Following these conventions makes classes predictable and compatible with IDEs, frameworks, and documentation tools.

question: Brom is tracing object construction. What does this print?
feedback: Each call to increment() adds 1 to the count field. After three calls, count is 3. get() returns the current value of count, so 3 is printed.

question: What does this toString override print?
feedback: When you pass an object to println, Java automatically calls its toString() method. The overridden toString here formats the coordinates as (x, y), producing (3, 7).

question: Aria is reviewing encapsulation. What does this print?
feedback: The constructor sets balance to 100.0. deposit(50.0) passes the guard and adds 50, making 150.0. deposit(-10.0) fails the amount > 0 check and is ignored. getBalance() returns 150.0.
